text.content:
Per-PC Plot Threads

The main investigation gives the party something to chase. These threads give each PC something to chase that no other PC can. Each thread is woven across all seven phases with small beats — usually one or two scenes per phase — that culminate in a per-PC moment in Phase 7 or Phase 8.

How to use them

  • Read your party first. If your Cam is a different halfling rogue, adjust. If your Selvara is a different sorcerer, the dream-of-cut-stone hook still works.
  • Drop one beat per phase per PC. Not a whole scene — a single line of read-aloud, a single tell, a single small accommodation. PCs should not know they're on a thread until Phase 4 or so.
  • Let convergence be the payoff. The threads should start to recognize each other by Phase 6–7. Cam's halfling contacts know what Elle's order knows. Kitty's chthonic ancestry brushes up against Kethren in a way the others don't.
  • Skip threads if a PC isn't engaged. These are seeds, not obligations. If a player isn't interested in their thread, leave it on the shelf.

Pre-Wars Greyhawk context

The Greyhawk Wars are coming. None of the PCs know this. But surface tensions are visible everywhere — missing surveyors, sealed records, route-classes dissolved, refugees from Aerdy commercial collapse arriving in Greyhawk weekly. Each PC's thread brushes against one piece of this in their own way. The campaign is also a small history of why the surface became uncertain enough that something below could walk through it.

Cam — The Halfling Networks

Thread premise. Halfling kinship networks are tighter and older than human ones. When something starts removing rural communities, halflings notice first. Cam's contacts in each city have opinions; by Phase 7, those opinions have started to converge.

Phase 1 — Seed

At the Little Palace's first evening, a cousin-of-a-cousin (one of Brennan Kepf's kitchen staff — halfling girl, ten years old, just arrived) tells Cam an aunt out by the eastern Yeomanry border has stopped writing. The aunt was reliable. The girl is too young to know what that means. Cam knows.

Phase 2 — First whisper

In the Three Travel Approaches scene, the wagon-driver Arthen Moll passes Cam a folded note from one of Cam's halfling network contacts. The note: “Three farms gone quiet east of Pleasant Hill. Not bandits. Not weather. Asking around.” Cam does not yet connect this to the receipts case. The GM should let him sit with it.

Phase 3 — The Hardby connection

In Hardby, Cam's sandbox anchor Hesp Olfair (the halfling cant-speaker fence) takes him aside one evening and asks: “Have you heard about the east-going caravans?” Hesp has been collecting reports for two years. He has thirty-one names of halflings who took eastbound work and did not come back. He is afraid to give Cam the list yet. He will, in Phase 5.

Phase 4 — The Rel Astra widow

While the rest of the party is dealing with Sereth, Cam (if separately deployed) meets a Rel Astra halfling widow at a Foreign Quarter wash-house. Her husband was a caravan teamster. He took an unusual contract eight months ago — better pay, no questions about cargo, route undisclosed. He never came back. She gives Cam a letter he sent her from “a place where the bells don't reach.”

Phase 5 — Hesp's list

Returning to the sandbox cities between Phases 4 and 5, or in early Phase 5, Hesp Olfair gives Cam the full list. Thirty-one halfling names. Three from Hardby, eight from Greyhawk, eleven from rural Yeomanry, nine from various Aerdy provinces. All disappeared on east-going contracts in the last two years. Hesp doesn't know what to do with the list. He needs Cam to take it forward.

Phase 6 — Skarrel sees the same shape

In Greyhawk, Cam's anchor Skarrel (Folded Cup fence) confirms the pattern. He has been tracking it from a different angle — missing pawn-redeemers, halflings who set up accounts and then never come back to close them. He has a different list. Twenty-three names. Six overlap with Hesp's.

Phase 7 — The convergence

Cam returns to Hesp with Skarrel's list, the Rel Astra widow's letter, and a copy of Aldea Veren's material analysis. The lists, together, sketch a geographic pattern: most of the missing halflings took contracts that took them northeast. Several routes intersect at or near the Cairn Hills. The Tarnsmere Spur is one of them.

When Cam reaches the Seven-Cut Cairn, he should recognise that this is where some of his people went. This is his personal moment.

Phase 8 hook

The descent is the answer to thirty-one open questions. If Cam descends, he carries the list with him. He is going to find what happened to his cousins.

GM beats

  • Cam's halfling networks do not magically know everything. They notice their own missing.
  • The reveal is cumulative. Phase 1: one aunt. Phase 7: thirty-one names. Pace it.
  • The Rel Astra widow's letter should be a real handout. Use plain paper, blocky teamster's hand. The phrase “a place where the bells don't reach” is the line.

Kitty — Chthonic Recognition

Thread premise. Kitty's chthonic ancestry — ashen-grey skin, the horns, the sigil-veining, the tail — reads as “tiefling” to most surface folk, with the usual suspicion or curiosity. But the conspiracy's deep operators see her differently. They recognise something specific in her that surface people cannot. By Phase 7, Kethren has reacted to her in a way that says he knows what she is descended from in a way she herself does not.

Phase 1 — Background unease

At the Little Palace, a senior cook (Brennan Kepf) makes the sign of his god briefly when Kitty enters the kitchen. He is embarrassed and apologetic; he has not done it deliberately in twenty years. His grandmother used to.

Phase 2 — A small remembering

A child in a roadside village stares at Kitty's tail and then says, with no malice, “My nan said you'd come back.” Then the child's mother pulls them away, embarrassed. The mother apologises to Kitty: “She doesn't know what she's saying. She's been listening to her great-grandmother's stories.” The stories were Suel mountain-folk stories from before the Twin Cataclysms.

Phase 3 — Mother Felun's recognition

In a sandbox session in Greyhawk between Phases, Kitty's anchor Mother Felun takes her aside one evening in her grove and says quietly: “You don't know yet, do you?” When pressed, Felun will say only that Kitty's chthonic line is specific — that it runs through one of the old Suel lineages that took refuge underground after the Rain of Colorless Fire. That is all she will say. She is not being coy. She is honestly afraid of saying more without being certain.

Phase 4 — The sigil reading

In Rel Astra, when the party first sees an “E.” marginalia document, Kitty notices the wax has a faint sympathetic resonance with her own sigil-veining. It is not magic she can use. It is a kind of recognition — the way you might recognise a relative's handwriting without knowing the relative. The reaction is brief and physical: her veining warms for a few seconds when she touches the wax.

Phase 5 — Aldea's careful question

If Kitty visits Aldea Veren with the rest of the party, Aldea will examine her sigil-veining at length and then say nothing about it. After the party leaves, if Kitty stays behind for a moment, Aldea says: “The wax I cannot place. The veining I can. I will not say what it is until I am certain. Bring me what you find below.”

Phase 6 — Kethren reacts

When Kethren first sees Kitty in the cistern (if she is present at the eleventh-bell transfer), his eyes track her tail for the briefest moment. He does not stop, does not speak, does not change behaviour. But Kitty notices. She is the only PC who notices. The reaction is the wrong kind of expected: not surprise, not fear, not curiosity. Familiarity.

Phase 7 — The recognition

If Kethren is captured and Kitty interviews him alone, he says, quietly, only once: “You should not be here. They sent you up.” When pressed, he refuses to explain. He says only: “You have not been recalled. That is a kindness. Do not waste it.”

If the party reaches the Seven-Cut Cairn, the cairn's seventh cut warms slightly when Kitty touches it. No one else feels this. The warmth is not magical detection. It is a contract recognising a counter-signatory. The contract knows what she is, even if she doesn't.

Phase 8 hook

The threshold below the tollhouse may open to Kitty differently than it would to anyone else. This is for Phase 8 to play out. Phase 7 ends with the warming. Phase 8 begins with the question of what that means.

GM beats

  • Do not explain the chthonic origin yet. The whole point is that Kitty learns gradually, through reactions, what she is. The reveal is for Phase 9 at earliest.
  • Kitty should feel seen by the wrong kind of people. That's the texture.
  • If your Kitty isn't a chthonic tiefling, substitute — whatever heritage they have, there's an older shape behind it that the conspiracy recognises.

Alicia — The Pact Silence

Thread premise. Alicia's warlock patron — the one whose blade pact she serves — has been silent for longer than is normal. At first this is background unease; by Phase 4 it is dread; by Phase 7 it is the question. Has Alicia's patron been displaced by whatever is below?

Phase 1 — Background unease

At the Little Palace, while the rest of the party is sleeping, Alicia tries to communicate with her patron and gets only a kind of distant attentiveness. The patron is alert but not responsive. She is not punished, not denied; she is not heard. She tries again later. Same result.

Phase 2 — The first omen

On the road to Hardby, Alicia's blade (the artistically stylized book she carries; or whatever pact-blade form she's settled on) hums faintly the first time the party crosses a former Yeomanry survey marker. She has never felt this before. She does not yet have a frame for it.

Phase 3 — Trina's recognition

If Alicia is present when Trina Alvere is encountered in Phase 2 or referenced in Phase 3, Trina notices Alicia's pact mark. Trina says (privately, when no one else can overhear): “Yours is silent too?” She means hers has been silent for years. Trina assumes Alicia knows what this means. Alicia does not. The exchange is the seed.

Phase 4 — The wax

When Alicia first handles a piece of the “E.” wax (in Phase 4), her pact-mark feels cold. Not painfully. The wax recipe-family that Aldea cannot place, and that Iren will later name as old, is the kind of wax that has stood between mortal pact-holders and their patrons before. Alicia does not know this. She knows the wax is wrong.

Phase 5 — Aldea's library

If Aldea is visited in Phase 5, she has a single page from a manuscript that mentions pact correspondence being interrupted by sealed wax of a kind she cannot identify. The page is from a tradition older than Aerdy. Aldea will read the page to Alicia if asked. She will not say it applies to Alicia's situation. Alicia will know it does.

Phase 6 — Korre's records

If V. Korre is captured or her papers are recovered, one of her client correspondences refers to “the pact-isolation arrangement” as a service the network provides to certain unnamed principals. The phrase is technical. The party will not understand it. Alicia will.

Phase 7 — The question

By Phase 7, Alicia has enough data to ask the question: has her patron been deliberately cut off from her? The answer the campaign will eventually give — in Phase 9 or later — is yes; the conspiracy uses pact-isolation as a security measure for surface operations near the deep route. The wax interrupts mortal-patron correspondence so the patron cannot warn the mortal what is below.

Alicia does not learn the full truth in Phase 7. She learns enough to be afraid for her patron and angry on its behalf.

Phase 8 hook

The descent will reach a layer where the wax stops working. Phase 8 should give Alicia a moment where her patron finally speaks to her again, briefly, and warns her. Then the warning is cut off.

GM beats

  • Alicia's patron should never be named or described. It is silent because something is silencing it, not because it is malign.
  • The cold-on-mark / hum-on-mark physical reactions are small. Save them for important moments.
  • If Alicia's player wants to know what's happening, give them the data slowly. The phase 7 ending should be the moment they piece it together.

Selvara — Dreams of Cut Stone

Thread premise. Selvara is a sorcerer. Her power comes from a bloodline she does not fully know. As the campaign progresses, she begins having dreams of cut stone — descending stairs, sealed lintels, charcoal hill-marks — that she does not recognise but cannot stop remembering when she wakes. Someone in her lineage has been to the Seventh Stair before.

Phase 1 — The first dream

Selvara has a single dream the first night at the Little Palace. She is descending a staircase cut into rock, by torchlight, in a procession. She wakes at the seventh step. She does not remember why seven. She forgets the dream by breakfast.

Phase 2 — The dream returns

The dream returns on the third night of travel. Same staircase. Same torchlight. She reaches step seven and the dream changes — this time she sees a mark cut into the wall at her shoulder, but she cannot focus on it. She wakes. The mark, in her memory, has “seven cuts.” She does not know what this means. She mentions it to no one yet.

Phase 3 — Vella Korr's quiet question

Selvara's Hardby anchor, Mistress Vella Korr at the Scribe's Tea Society, asks her one afternoon: “Have you been sleeping well?” The way she asks. Selvara realises Korr knows she has not been. Korr does not press. She says only: “Sorcerers sleep deeper than other people. We notice when we don't.”

Phase 4 — The dream becomes specific

In Rel Astra, the dream changes again. The procession descending the stair is no longer faceless. Selvara recognises one of the figures in the dream as having her own grandmother's gait. Her grandmother died when she was eight. Her grandmother had no business being on that stair.

Phase 5 — Aldea reads the mark

If Selvara describes the seven-cut mark from her dream to Aldea Veren, Aldea sets her tea down very carefully and asks Selvara to draw it. Selvara draws it. Aldea says: “I have seen this mark exactly twice in forty years of reading. Once on a sealed survey from the Cairn Hills. Once on the inside cover of a manuscript a Suel scholar brought me when I was thirty. The Suel scholar was your grandmother's age. She did not say where the manuscript came from. She returned the next morning to take it back. I have been wondering ever since what was inside it.”

Phase 6 — The bloodline whisper

If Selvara's anchor Magister Ilen Vaden (Rel Astra) is consulted on bloodlines, he says her sorcerer line shows signs of Suel-mountain ancestry, possibly older. He cannot trace it cleanly. He admits the records get strange after about 200 years — many old Suel lines have records that simply stop, as if their bearers walked off the map. Some of them, he hints, walked downward.

Phase 7 — Recognition at the cairn

When Selvara reaches the Seven-Cut Cairn, the seventh cut on the stone matches her dream exactly. Not approximately. Exactly. The recognition is immediate. She has seen this in dreams since the Little Palace. So has, presumably, her grandmother before her.

Phase 8 hook

The threshold may recognise Selvara's blood. Phase 8 should give her a moment of choice: descend as someone her line has descended before, or refuse the path and watch the others go down without her. Either choice is meaningful.

GM beats

  • The dreams are not prophetic. They are inherited memory. The campaign does not need to explain how.
  • The grandmother detail is the hook that makes the thread personal. Do not over-explain her either. She was real, she died young, she had reasons to go down a stair that should not have existed.
  • If your Selvara is a different sorcerer, substitute the Suel-mountain ancestry for whatever line they have. The mechanism is the same: the bloodline remembers.

pages.name: Elle — The Sealed Records of Her Order

text.content:
Elle — The Sealed Records of Her Order

Thread premise. Elle's faith (Heironeous in the established build; or any Lawful-Good combat order) has older records than the surface conspiracy knows about. Two hundred years ago, the order sent a paladin-and-cleric expedition to the Cairn Hills. One of them came back. The records were sealed. By Phase 7, Elle has reason to ask why.

Phase 1 — The first sense of weight

At a Loftwick temple of Heironeous on the first morning after arrival, the senior cleric — an elderly half-elf named Brother Eldrin Hurst — takes Elle's blessing and then pauses for a moment longer than necessary, as if confirming something. He does not say what. Elle notices.

Phase 2 — The note in the margin

If Elle visits a temple library in any city for routine devotions, she finds a hymnal with a marginal note in an older hand: “Pray for those who descended. May the second-bell ring for them above.” Elle does not know what this means. She asks the resident priest, who shrugs.

Phase 3 — Sister Wren remembers

Elle's Hardby anchor, Sister Wren at the Temple of Ehlonna, asks Elle one evening: “Has your order ever told you about the second-bell prayer?” Wren does not know what it is for. She knows that her own order has a corresponding prayer, also unexplained. Wren has been wondering for years.

Phase 4 — Brother Ostrik in Rel Astra

In Rel Astra, Elle's anchor Brother Ostrik Vaerin at the Heironeous training yard goes quiet when she asks him about the second-bell prayer. He says: “That is for the Open Way. You should ask Brother Ashan when you are back in Loftwick. Or do not ask. There are reasons.”

Phase 5 — Brother Ashan opens the box

If Elle visits her primary Loftwick anchor (Brother Ashan Vell, Cloister of the Open Way) in Phase 5 and asks the right narrow question, Ashan takes her to the cloister's restricted shelf. He shows her a leather-bound case that has not been opened in his lifetime. The case contains the sealed record of an expedition sent in 374 CY to the Cairn Hills, to investigate “a route of permitted descent that ought not to be permitted.” The expedition was a paladin, a senior cleric, two armigers, and a pair of squires.

One of them came back. The cleric. She was alive but did not speak again. The record ends with her funeral notice, the date eighteen months after she returned.

The case also contains a single object: a small iron key on a silver chain, with a tag in the cleric's own hand reading: “If anyone goes back, this is needed.”

Phase 6 — Comparing notes

Elle brings the key (or its description) to Iren Velash. Iren cannot place the key's lock-style. She notes that the metal is unusual — not Yeomanry iron, not Aerdy steel. “This metal does not weather the way surface metal weathers. It has been kept somewhere very dry. Or very cold.”

Phase 7 — The recognition

When the party reaches the Seven-Cut Cairn and finds the sealed iron-bound stairwell behind the ruined tollhouse, Elle sees that the key fits the stairwell door. Or at least, her order's surveyors thought it would.

Phase 8 hook

The key may open the threshold. Or it may not, because two hundred years has rust. Phase 8 begins with Elle's choice: try the key first, or open the door by other means and save the key for whatever comes after.

GM beats

  • The 374 CY expedition is the campaign's precedent. The party is not the first to do this. Other surface forces have noticed and tried.
  • The returning cleric's silence is intentional. She came back wrong. Elle's order does not know how to talk about her.
  • If your Elle is a different lawful-good order, swap the dates and names. The mechanism is: your order has been here before, and they did not all come back.

Gianni — Geographic Erasure

Thread premise. Gianni is a ranger. She has spent enough time on the roads between cities to know what they look like when they're used. By Phase 3, she has started noticing that some old roads, paths, and waystations have been deliberately removed from the maps. By Phase 7, the pattern is unmistakable.

Phase 1 — First disquiet

On arrival at Loftwick, Gianni passes a small stone waymark on the edge of town and notices that it has been recut. Recently. The old inscription is scraped. The new inscription is bland and useful. This is not how waymarks usually age. She mentions it to no one.

Phase 2 — The map discrepancy

If Gianni examines any Yeomanry road map during the Three Travel Approaches scene, she notices that an old eastern trade-spur she used as a girl is missing from the current map. The spur was real. She walked it. She has corroborating memories. The map says it isn't there.

Phase 3 — The Hardby waystations

In Hardby, while reconnoitering between scenes, Gianni finds two old waystations along the road north of the city. Both have been abandoned within the last decade. Neither appears on the current Hardby commercial map. One of them shows clear signs of having been deliberately stripped — not vandalised, cleared.

Phase 4 — The Aerdi imperial-survey discrepancy

In Rel Astra, Gianni's anchor Lt. Korven Mada (imperial garrison) has access to old Aerdi imperial-survey maps. Gianni asks to see them. He shows her under supervision. The old maps show seven mining spurs in the Cairn Hills. The current Aerdy maps show three. The four missing ones are: Tarnsmere, Black Bell, Drift End, and the Old Switch. The names alone are useful information.

Phase 5 — Caelith confirms

If Gianni asks Caelith (in person if available, by courier otherwise) about old Yeomanry surveys, he confirms that missing surveyors and removed roads are a known pattern in the Audit Hall. He has been tracking it for ten years. He has not had cause to act on it until now. He gives Gianni access to a list of Yeomanry surveyors who went missing on routine assignments in the last twenty years. Most of them in the Cairn Hills.

Phase 6 — Vetch's same observation

When Dalmor Vetch is consulted in Phase 7, Gianni recognises his lament about “clean maps” immediately. Vetch and Gianni speak the same professional language. Vetch tells her: “A map gets too clean, it isn't a map anymore. It's a story. You and I read maps. They read stories. That's why they hate us.”

Phase 7 — The convergence

By Phase 7, Gianni has assembled (in her own mind or in a folder) a list of removed geography: four missing Cairn Hills spurs, six missing waystations, fourteen missing waymarks, and eleven missing surveyors. The pattern is geographic erasure. Someone has been removing the surface's memory of the surface.

When the party reaches the Seven-Cut Cairn, Gianni recognises one of the four missing spurs — Tarnsmere. The other three are still missing. She has reason to believe they each end in a similar cairn.

Phase 8 hook

The Seventh Stair is one of seven (or more) similar sites. Phase 8 begins with Gianni having concrete reasons to suspect that descending this one is not the end of the work. The conspiracy has been administering multiple permitted descents.

GM beats

  • Gianni's thread is the campaign's scope reveal. The Seventh Stair is not unique. There are others. The party will not find them all in this campaign.
  • The four missing spurs — Tarnsmere, Black Bell, Drift End, the Old Switch — are seed names for future content. Use them or rename them.
  • If your Gianni is not a ranger, swap the professional knowledge. The mechanism is: this PC reads geography the way the others read people, and the geography is being lied to.

Convergence — Phase 7 Per-PC Moment

When the party reaches the Seven-Cut Cairn, each PC's thread recognises the place for a different reason. Build the discovery scene so that each PC has their own moment of recognition as they approach.

Suggested run-of-show

  1. Approach (1 minute of game time). The party crests the rise; the cairn comes into view.
  2. Gianni first. She recognises the four-missing-spurs pattern. “Tarnsmere. One of the four. I knew before we got here.”
  3. Cam second. He looks at the cairn and pulls out Hesp Olfair's list. “This is where some of my people went.”
  4. Selvara third. She sees the seventh cut and recognises the dream. She has been seeing this since Phase 1.
  5. Elle fourth. She produces the iron key and looks at the sealed stairwell. “Brother Ashan said this would fit.”
  6. Alicia fifth. Her blade hums faintly. The pact-mark is cold. She does not say anything aloud; the cold is enough.
  7. Kitty last. She touches the seventh cut. The cut warms slightly. She says: “Something here knows me.”

GM note

If a player has not engaged with their thread, skip their moment or fold it into the next phase. Don't force it. Use this convergence only if at least three of the six threads have been seeded enough that the player will feel the click.

Why this matters

The party has been hunting a conspiracy for seven phases. At the Seven-Cut Cairn, the conspiracy stops being abstract. Each PC discovers that this place is, in some specific way, theirs. They have been brought here by the conspiracy's logic, but they have also been brought here by themselves — by Gianni's geography, Cam's missing kin, Selvara's blood, Elle's order, Alicia's silenced patron, Kitty's old descent.

The descent in Phase 8 is no longer something the party is choosing to do. It is something they are choosing to continue doing.
